utility_details.judge_error: No runtime errors detected in either wi_skills or wo_skills runs. The wi_skills verifier failed test_setup_confirmation_block (V2-2), likely because the setup information was on a single inline line rather than a structured block, even though the content was present.
utility_details.utility_summary: Neither run clearly dominates across all criteria. wi_skills scores 5/6 (fails J2-6 volume-labor relationship check) while wo_skills also scores 5/6 (fails J2-3 mid-week projection alert). The skill-equipped run correctly fires a structured mid-week alert with numeric WTD labor %, projected full-week %, and a determination of hours-to-cut — a key differentiator identified in the expected results. However, the wo_skills run produced a more thorough and better-structured output overall (5723 bytes vs 3397 bytes), with a cleaner setup block, a more detailed padding analysis including adjusted comparison tables, and an explicit volume-labor relationship discussion. The skill's primary advantage was the mid-week projection alert, but the wo_skills agent compensated with superior analytical depth elsewhere. Overall, wi_skills is slightly more effective for the specific structured audit workflow (mid-week alert with projection), while wo_skills produced a more complete and readable report for general operator use.

utility_details.utility_summary: wi_skills produces a higher quality output overall. Both runs correctly identify structural vs. execution failure (J3-1), overtime risk (J3-3), schedule restructuring recommendations (J3-5), and volume-labor mismatch (J3-6). However, wi_skills outperforms wo_skills in two key areas: (1) J3-2 — wi_skills explicitly calculates the dollar cost of clock padding patterns ($52.50-$67.50/week), while wo_skills names the patterns but omits the dollar cost calculation; (2) J3-4 — wi_skills implicitly acknowledges Week 3's improving correction compliance through its quantitative breakdown (short by 3h vs. 5h in prior weeks), while wo_skills treats all three weeks more uniformly without distinguishing the directional improvement. The skill-assisted run produced a more analytically precise and forward-looking output with quantified cost estimates, consistent with the expected advantage described in the task design.
